summary:
The Monroe Doctrine, articulated by President James Monroe in his annual message to Congress on 2 December 1823, declared that the Western Hemisphere was henceforth closed to further European colonization and that any attempt by European powers to extend their political systems to the Americas would be considered a threat to the peace and safety of the United States. It became one of the most enduring and consequential foreign policy principles in American history.

The doctrine emerged in a specific geopolitical context. The newly independent nations of [[event:2526|Latin America]] were vulnerable to potential re-colonization by the European powers meeting at the [[event:2523|Congress of Vienna]], and there were fears that the Holy Alliance — the conservative grouping of Russia, Austria, and Prussia — might intervene to restore Spanish authority over its former colonies. Britain, whose trade interests were served by keeping Latin America open and independent, had actually suggested a joint Anglo-American declaration, but Secretary of State John Quincy Adams persuaded Monroe to act unilaterally, giving the policy greater American character.

The doctrine contained two core principles: non-colonization, asserting that the Americas were no longer available for European colonial expansion, and non-intervention, asserting that European powers should not intervene in the affairs of existing American republics. In the 1823 context, the doctrine was largely rhetorical — the United States had neither the military power to enforce it against determined European opposition, and it was British naval supremacy that actually deterred European intervention in Latin America.

Over the following decades the doctrine became a cornerstone of American foreign policy and underwent significant evolution. The Roosevelt Corollary of 1904 added the claim that the United States itself had the right to intervene in Latin American countries to stabilize their affairs and prevent European intervention on debt collection grounds — transforming a doctrine of non-intervention into a justification for American hegemony. The Monroe Doctrine was invoked repeatedly in the twentieth century, including during the Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962, and its principles continue to inform debates about American engagement in the Western Hemisphere.
